meta.name: AUDIT Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test
meta.description: The AUDIT is a 10-item screening tool developed by the World Health Organization (WHO) to identify hazardous drinking patterns and possible alcohol use disorders. It assesses alcohol consumption, drinking dependence symptoms, and alcohol-related harm.
meta.author: Saunders et al. (1993)
meta.reference: Saunders JB, Aasland OG, Babor TF, de la Fuente JR, Grant M. Development of the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT): WHO Collaborative Project on Early Detection of Persons with Harmful Alcohol Use-II. Addiction. 1993;88(6):791-804.
meta.instruction: The following questions ask about your alcohol use during the past year. Please select the answer that best describes your situation.

questions.text: How often do you have a drink containing alcohol?
How many standard drinks do you have on a typical day when you are drinking?
How often do you have six or more drinks on one occasion?
How often during the last year have you found that you were not able to stop drinking once you had started?
How often during the last year have you failed to do what was normally expected of you because of drinking?
How often during the last year have you needed a first drink in the morning to get yourself going after a heavy drinking session?
How often during the last year have you had a feeling of guilt or remorse after drinking?
How often during the last year have you been unable to remember what happened the night before because of your drinking?
Have you or someone else been injured because of your drinking?

choices.label: Yes, but not in the last year

questions.text: Has a relative, friend, doctor, or other health care worker been concerned about your drinking or suggested that you cut down?

choices.label: Yes, but not in the last year

ranges.label: Low Risk
ranges.description: Your score indicates low-risk alcohol consumption. Your current drinking patterns do not appear to be causing significant health or social problems. Maintaining awareness of your alcohol use is a positive health behavior.
suggestions: Continue to drink within recommended guidelines if you choose to drink
Be aware of situations that might lead to increased consumption
Know the recommended limits: no more than 2 standard drinks per day for men, 1 for women

ranges.label: Hazardous Drinking
ranges.description: Your score suggests hazardous alcohol use that increases your risk for health problems, injuries, and social difficulties. At this level, your drinking pattern may not yet have caused significant harm, but it exceeds safe limits and warrants attention.
suggestions: Consider reducing your alcohol consumption to lower-risk levels
Track your drinking patterns to identify triggers and high-risk situations
Set specific goals for reducing the quantity and frequency of drinking
Your healthcare provider can offer brief advice and support for cutting back
Avoid binge drinking episodes, which carry acute health risks

ranges.label: Harmful Drinking
ranges.description: Your score indicates harmful alcohol use that is likely already causing damage to your physical health, mental well-being, or social relationships. At this level, alcohol is probably interfering with your daily functioning and may be leading to dependence.
suggestions: Consult your healthcare provider about your alcohol use for a thorough evaluation
Consider structured brief intervention or counseling programs
Reduce your alcohol consumption and track your progress
Be aware of withdrawal symptoms if you have been drinking heavily; seek medical advice before suddenly stopping
Consider involving a supportive family member or friend in your plan to cut back

ranges.label: Possible Alcohol Dependence
ranges.description: Your score strongly suggests alcohol dependence, a serious medical condition that typically requires professional treatment. At this level, you may experience tolerance, withdrawal symptoms, loss of control over drinking, and significant impairment in daily life.
suggestions: Seek professional help from a specialist in addiction medicine or substance use disorders
Do not attempt to stop drinking suddenly without medical supervision, as alcohol withdrawal can be medically dangerous
Treatment options include medically supervised detoxification, counseling, and medications that reduce cravings
Support groups such as AA or SMART Recovery can provide ongoing support
Recovery from alcohol dependence is possible; many people achieve long-term sobriety with proper help
